corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: In this paper, we present a framework for self-supervised learning of representations from raw audio data.

context_window.previous: This has been particularly successful for natural language processing [ 43 , 45 , 9 ] and is an active research area for computer vision [20, 2, 36, 19, 6].
context_window.current: In this paper, we present a framework for self-supervised learning of representations from raw audio data.
context_window.next: Our approach encodes speech audio via a multi-layer convolutional neural network and then masks spans of the resulting latent speech representations [ 26 , 56 ], similar to masked language modeling [ 9 ].

location.sentence: Learning purely from labeled examples does not resemble language acquisition in humans: infants learn language by listening to adults around them - a process that requires learning good representations of speech.

context_window.previous: However, in many settings labeled data is much harder to come by than unlabeled data: current speech recognition systems require thousands of hours of transcribed speech to reach acceptable performance which is not available for the vast majority of the nearly 7,000 languages spoken worldwide [ 31 ].
context_window.current: Learning purely from labeled examples does not resemble language acquisition in humans: infants learn language by listening to adults around them - a process that requires learning good representations of speech.
context_window.next: In machine learning, self-supervised learning has emerged as a paradigm to learn general data representations from unlabeled examples and to fine-tune the model on labeled data.

top_candidates.sentence: The LARGE model pre-trained on LV-60k and fine-tuned on only 10 minutes of labeled data achieves a word error rate of 5.2/8.6 on the Librispeech clean/other test sets.
A recent iterative self-training approach represents the state of the art on the clean 100 hour subset of Librispeech but it requires multiple iterations of labeling, filtering, and re-training.

On the 100 hour subset of Librispeech, their method achieves WER 4.2/8.6 on test-clean/other which compares to WER 2.3/5.0 with the LARGE model in a like for like setup, a relative WER reduction of 45%/42%.
When the LARGE model uses an order of magnitude less labeled data (10h labeled), then it still achieves WER 3.2/6.1, an error reduction of 24%/29% relative to iterative self-training.
Using only a single hour of labeled data, the same model achieves WER 3.9/7.6 which improves on both test-clean and test-other by 7%/12% -with two orders of magnitude less labeled data.
Table shows that our approach achieves WER 1.8/3.3 on test-clean/other on the full Librispeech benchmark.

This is despite a weaker baseline architecture: supervised training of our architecture achieves WER 2.1/4.6 (LARGE -from scratch) compared to WER 1.9/4.1 for ContextNet , the baseline architecture of the state of the art .

This is despite a weaker baseline architecture: supervised training of our architecture achieves WER 2.1/4.6 (LARGE -from scratch) compared to WER 1.9/4.1 for ContextNet , the baseline architecture of the state of the art .

Predicting only up to one time step performs poorly because there is little training signal from each utterance and predicting more time steps performs better but does not significantly outperform predicting all masked time steps.
We consider two types of language models (LM): a 4-gram model and a Transformer trained on the Librispeech LM corpus.
As unlabeled data we consider the Librispeech corpus without transcriptions containing 960 hours of audio (LS-960) or the audio data from LibriVox (LV-60k).
We fine-tune the pre-trained models for phoneme recognition on the TIMIT dataset .
Table shows that our approach achieves WER 1.8/3.3 on test-clean/other on the full Librispeech benchmark.
The training accuracy of identifying the correct latent audio representation increases from 62% to 78.0% when switching from quantized to continuous targets.

We show for the first time that learning powerful representations from speech audio alone followed by fine-tuning on transcribed speech can outperform the best semi-supervised methods while being conceptually simpler. wav2vec 2.0 masks the speech input in the latent space and solves a contrastive task defined over a quantization of the latent representations which are jointly learned.
When lowering the amount of labeled data to one hour, wav2vec 2.0 outperforms the previous state of the art on the 100 hour subset while using 100 times less labeled data.
When lowering the amount of labeled data to one hour, wav2vec 2.0 outperforms the previous state of the art on the 100 hour subset while using 100 times less labeled data.
Using just ten minutes of labeled data and pre-training on 53k hours of unlabeled data still achieves 4.8/8.2 WER.
In this paper, we present a framework for self-supervised learning of representations from raw audio data.
In this paper, we present a framework for self-supervised learning of representations from raw audio data.
Our results show that jointly learning discrete speech units with contextualized representations achieves substantially better results than fixed units learned in a prior step .

We also demonstrate the feasibility of ultra-low resource speech recognition: when using only 10 minutes of labeled data, our approach achieves word error rate (WER) 4.8/8.2 on the clean/other test sets of Librispeech.
We set a new state of the art on TIMIT phoneme recognition as well as the 100 hour clean subset of Librispeech.

Moreover, when we lower the amount of labeled data to just one hour, we still outperform the previous state of the art self-training method of while using 100 times less labeled data and the same amount of unlabeled data.

Moreover, when we lower the amount of labeled data to just one hour, we still outperform the previous state of the art self-training method of while using 100 times less labeled data and the same amount of unlabeled data.
